refined_fr.refined_fr: Enable measurement and reporting of material throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the system's capability to measure/report throughput metrics, and (2) the achievement of a customer-defined target. The latter is a success criterion dependent on operational execution, not a system requirement. The refined FR isolates the system's functional obligation—providing visibility into throughput—which is solution-neutral and testable independent of whether a specific productivity target is met. The target itself belongs in acceptance criteria or KPI definitions, not in the FR.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the NGHE solution captures and reports material throughput (m³/h or ton/h) during representative operations. Verify data accuracy against independent measurement. Confirm reporting is accessible to Site Manager and Equipment Operator in real time or near-real time.
sub_frs: Acquire material throughput data (volume or mass per time interval) during active operations.
Calculate and aggregate throughput metrics in customer-specified units (m³/h or ton/h).
Display or report throughput metrics to authorized stakeholders in real time or near-real time.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire instantaneous flow rate via continuous sensing of material stream properties (mass or volume per unit time) and integrate over evaluation period to compute cumulative throughput
DP2: Measure discrete material batches (count, mass, or volume per batch) at regular intervals and extrapolate to throughput rate over evaluation period
DP3: Monitor material level change in a bounded reservoir (input hopper or output stockpile) over time and infer net throughput from level differential and material density
DP4: Combine multiple independent measurement modalities (e.g., batch counting + continuous sensing) with cross-validation logic to report throughput with confidence bounds
DP5: Establish a calibrated reference baseline (e.g., manual tally or certified third-party measurement) and report relative deviation from baseline as throughput confidence metric
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1: Acquire instantaneous flow rate via continuous sensing of material stream properties (mass or volume per unit time) and integrate over evaluation period to compute cumulative throughput

potential_sources: Sensor placement dependency: flow rate accuracy depends on material homogeneity and stream geometry at sensing point; poor placement couples measurement to upstream process variability
Calibration coupling: instantaneous flow rate requires periodic recalibration; drift in sensor response couples measurement accuracy to maintenance schedule
Integration method coupling: numerical integration method (trapezoidal, Euler, etc.) couples reported throughput to sampling frequency and noise filtering strategy

Environmental sensitivity: material properties (density, moisture, particle size distribution) may vary with ambient conditions; sensing modality must be decoupled from these variations or explicitly account for them
coupling_check.notes: DP1 is preferred because it provides continuous, real-time feedback and avoids the discrete sampling bias of DP2 and the reservoir-dependent assumptions of DP3. Coupling risks are manageable through sensor placement standards, calibration protocols, and environmental compensation. DP4 and DP5 add complexity without addressing the core FR; they are secondary validation strategies, not primary solutions.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Material stream is accessible for continuous sensing without disrupting operations. Risk: If access is limited or sensing introduces friction/blockage, alternative modalities (DP2, DP3) may be required.

Assumption: Sensor drift and environmental variation are predictable and correctable via calibration. Risk: If material properties change rapidly or unpredictably, real-time compensation may be infeasible; DP4 (multi-modal) becomes necessary.

Assumption: Evaluation period is long enough (relative to sensor noise and integration error) to meet acceptance criterion (reported ≥ productivity_target). Risk: Short evaluation windows amplify measurement noise; acceptance criterion may be unachievable without high-fidelity sensing.

Assumption: Worksite safety plan permits sensor installation and maintenance in the material stream. Risk: If safety constraints prohibit intrusive sensing, non-contact modalities (e.g., level-based DP3) must be substituted.

Assumption: Material throughput is quasi-steady during evaluation period. Risk: If throughput exhibits large transients or step changes, instantaneous flow rate sensing (DP1) may not capture representative average; batch-based DP2 may be more robust.

Assumption: Reported throughput is defined as net material exiting the system (not internal recirculation or losses). Risk: If losses are significant and unmeasured, reported throughput will overstate actual productivity; DP3 (level-based) may be more accurate if input and output are both monitored.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR should be refined to clarify: (1) Is throughput net output or gross input? (2) What is the acceptable measurement uncertainty (e.g., ±5% of productivity_target)? (3) Are there constraints on sensor placement, intrusiveness, or maintenance frequency? (4) What is the minimum evaluation period and expected variability in material properties? With these clarifications, DP1 can be decomposed into sub-FRs for sensor selection (IM), calibration protocol (IM), and integration algorithm (IM), each with explicit acceptance criteria. If measurement uncertainty or environmental variability is high, recommend DP4 (multi-modal with cross-validation) as a more robust alternative.

im_boundary.name: Vehicle
im_boundary.scope: Physical and logical boundary encompassing all onboard sensing, actuation, and real-time data acquisition systems including flow rate measurement hardware, sensor integration, signal conditioning, and local cumulative throughput computation.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle transmits instantaneous flow rate and cumulative throughput measurements to external systems (NGHE Solution, Teleoperation) at periodic intervals or on-demand.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle receives evaluation period parameters, sensor calibration commands, and flow measurement mode directives from external control systems.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle communicates flow rate data and receives remote configuration updates via network connectivity.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment. Flow sensing hardware, sensor signal acquisition, integration logic, and cumulative computation are naturally co-located in vehicle systems. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry export and tasking input cross boundaries. This is the primary locus of physical sensing and real-time measurement.

Poor containment. NGHE Solution is a high-level orchestration/planning layer. Placing continuous sensing and integration logic here violates separation of concerns. Sensing hardware remains on Vehicle; computation would be duplicated or remotely executed, creating latency and coupling issues. Strong diffusion across Vehicle and network.

Very poor containment. Teleoperation is a human-in-the-loop control interface, not a sensing/measurement subsystem. Flow rate acquisition and integration belong on the Vehicle itself. Placing this DP in Teleoperation creates tight coupling to remote operator actions and introduces unacceptable latency for continuous sensing. High diffusion.

Poor containment. Safety Services is a cross-cutting concern for hazard monitoring and alert generation. While flow rate data may feed safety logic, the acquisition and integration of flow measurements is not a safety service responsibility. Sensing hardware and computation remain on Vehicle. Significant diffusion and role confusion.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Vehicle module is the natural and optimal boundary for DP1 because: (1) it owns the physical sensing hardware (flow sensors, transducers); (2) it performs real-time signal acquisition and conditioning; (3) it executes the integration algorithm locally with minimal latency; (4) it maintains state (cumulative throughput) across the evaluation period; (5) it exports results via Telemetry and accepts configuration via Tasking, keeping external coupling minimal and well-defined.
containment_risks: Risk: If flow sensor hardware is physically located on a separate subsystem (e.g., a remote probe or external measurement station), the boundary may need to be reconsidered or extended to include that hardware as part of Vehicle's sensing infrastructure.

Risk: If cumulative throughput computation requires real-time coordination with multiple distributed sensors or external calibration services, diffusion may increase and a shared service module may become necessary.
Risk: If the evaluation period is dynamically set by external systems and requires frequent synchronization, Tasking interface coupling may increase, but this remains acceptable as a control input.
Risk: Network latency in Telemetry export could delay visibility of flow rate data to NGHE Solution; if real-time feedback is critical, a higher-bandwidth or lower-latency interface may be needed.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The Vehicle module owns the physical sensing hardware, signal acquisition, and integration logic. Verification can be conducted via direct measurement of flow rate outputs against calibrated reference standards during representative operations. Cumulative throughput can be validated against known material volumes dispensed over the evaluation period. Acceptance criterion (reported productivity ≥ productivity_target) is directly measurable from Vehicle telemetry exports.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The FR is allocated entirely to the Vehicle IM boundary. External systems (NGHE Solution, Teleoperation, Safety Services) consume the measurement results via Telemetry interface but do not participate in acquisition or integration. Tasking interface provides configuration inputs (evaluation period, calibration commands, measurement mode) but does not distribute the core DP logic.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP1 directly and sufficiently addresses the FR. Continuous sensing of material stream properties (mass or volume per unit time) with integration over the evaluation period is the correct logical approach to compute cumulative throughput. The DP is technology-agnostic and does not over-specify implementation details (e.g., sensor type, integration algorithm variant). It is decomposable into sub-DPs if needed (e.g., sensor selection, signal conditioning, numerical integration method) but at this level of abstraction, it is sound and complete.

diffusion_risks: Risk: If flow sensor hardware is physically mounted on a separate subsystem (e.g., remote probe, external measurement station, or third-party equipment), the Vehicle IM boundary must be explicitly extended or redefined to include that hardware as part of its sensing infrastructure. Current assumption is that all sensing hardware is Vehicle-resident.

Risk: If cumulative throughput computation requires real-time synchronization with multiple distributed sensors (e.g., multiple flow points, redundant measurement channels) or external calibration services, diffusion may increase. Mitigation: ensure all distributed sensors are logically aggregated within Vehicle's integration logic; external calibration is a one-time or periodic Tasking input, not a real-time dependency.

Risk: If evaluation period parameters are dynamically modified by external systems during an active measurement cycle, Tasking interface coupling increases and state management complexity grows. Mitigation: define clear semantics for evaluation period changes (e.g., reset vs. extend) and ensure Vehicle maintains authoritative state.

Risk: Network latency in Telemetry export could delay visibility of instantaneous flow rate to NGHE Solution and Teleoperation. If real-time feedback is critical for operational decisions, a higher-bandwidth or lower-latency interface (e.g., direct CAN bus, dedicated telemetry channel) may be required. Current design assumes periodic or on-demand export is acceptable.

Risk: If Safety Services requires sub-second latency on flow rate anomaly detection (e.g., blockage, overflow), relying on Telemetry export may introduce unacceptable delay. Mitigation: implement local safety logic on Vehicle (e.g., threshold-based alerts) and export only alerts via Telemetry, not raw flow data.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score). The Vehicle module is the natural and optimal boundary for DP1. It owns the physical sensing hardware, performs real-time signal acquisition and conditioning, executes the integration algorithm locally with minimal latency, and maintains cumulative throughput state. External coupling is minimal and well-defined: Telemetry exports results; Tasking provides configuration. No significant diffusion detected. NGHE Solution, Teleoperation, and Safety Services are correctly excluded from the core DP realization; they consume measurement outputs as needed. The IM boundary aligns with physical and logical system architecture.

recommended_revisions: Clarify sensor hardware ownership: explicitly document whether all flow measurement hardware (transducers, signal conditioning circuits, analog-to-digital converters) is Vehicle-resident or if any components are external. If external, extend the Vehicle IM boundary definition or establish a clear interface contract.

Define evaluation period semantics: specify how the Vehicle handles dynamic changes to evaluation period parameters received via Tasking (e.g., reset cumulative counter, extend period, start new measurement cycle). Document state transitions and any constraints on timing.

Specify Telemetry export frequency and latency: define the periodic interval or on-demand trigger for flow rate and cumulative throughput transmission. If real-time feedback is required by NGHE Solution or Safety Services, justify the chosen latency and bandwidth.

Establish local safety thresholds on Vehicle: if Safety Services requires sub-second anomaly detection, implement threshold-based alerts (e.g., flow rate out of range, integration error) on the Vehicle itself and export only alerts via Telemetry, not raw data streams.
Document sensor calibration procedure: specify how calibration commands received via Tasking are applied to the flow measurement hardware and how calibration state is maintained and verified.

Verify acceptance criterion traceability: confirm that the productivity_target and evaluation period are defined in the worksite operational policies and that Vehicle's cumulative throughput computation is calibrated to match the agreed measurement standard (e.g., volumetric units, mass units, time basis).

decision.justification: Gate results confirm FR1 is well-formed, DP1 is logically sound, and Vehicle IM is the correct containment boundary with excellent (0.92) alignment. However, safety criticality is HIGH, and the diffusion_risks identify critical sub-system concerns—particularly real-time anomaly detection, sensor hardware ownership clarity, and evaluation period state management—that require decomposition into sub-FRs to ensure safety-critical requirements are explicitly addressed and allocated. The recommended_revisions indicate that DP1, while sound at this abstraction level, is decomposable into sub-DPs (sensor selection, signal conditioning, integration algorithm, safety thresholds, calibration management) that map to distinct sub-FRs. Decomposition is warranted to make safety-critical concerns explicit and traceable.
next_level_sub_frs: FR1.1: Acquire continuous flow rate measurements from material stream with sensor accuracy and sampling frequency sufficient to detect anomalies (blockage, overflow, sensor failure) within safety-critical latency bounds (< 1 second for Safety Services threshold detection).

FR1.2: Condition and integrate flow rate signals in real-time to compute cumulative throughput over the evaluation period with numerical stability and error bounds acceptable for productivity acceptance criterion (±5% or per worksite policy).

FR1.3: Detect and alert on flow rate anomalies (out-of-range, rate-of-change violations, integration errors) locally on Vehicle with sub-second latency to enable Safety Services intervention without relying on Telemetry export latency.

FR1.4: Manage evaluation period state (start, reset, extend, terminate) in response to Tasking commands with clear semantics and atomic state transitions to prevent measurement inconsistency or data loss.

FR1.5: Calibrate flow measurement hardware (sensor offset, gain, nonlinearity correction) in response to Tasking calibration commands and maintain calibration state with traceability for acceptance verification.

FR1.6: Export cumulative throughput and instantaneous flow rate to NGHE Solution and Teleoperation via Telemetry interface with defined frequency and latency; export anomaly alerts to Safety Services with priority queuing.

revise.why: DP1 is logically correct but under-specifies the safety-critical sub-functions. Revise DP1 to explicitly decompose into: (DP1.1) real-time anomaly detection with local thresholds, (DP1.2) numerical integration with error bounds, (DP1.3) state machine for evaluation period management, (DP1.4) calibration state machine, (DP1.5) Telemetry export scheduler with priority handling. This decomposition makes safety-critical concerns explicit and enables allocation of sub-DPs to sub-IMs (e.g., safety logic on Vehicle firmware vs. data export on Telemetry module).

assumptions: All flow measurement hardware (transducers, signal conditioning, ADC) is Vehicle-resident; if external, Vehicle IM boundary must be extended or interface contract established.
Safety Services requires sub-second latency for anomaly detection; local threshold-based alerts on Vehicle are acceptable; Telemetry export latency is not safety-critical.
Evaluation period parameters (start time, duration, reset semantics) are defined in worksite operational policies and communicated via Tasking interface.
Sensor calibration is performed offline or during maintenance windows; calibration state is persistent and verified before measurement cycles.
Productivity acceptance criterion (reported productivity ≥ productivity_target) is defined in worksite policy and matches Vehicle's cumulative throughput measurement standard (units, time basis).
NGHE Solution and Teleoperation can tolerate periodic (e.g., 1 Hz) or on-demand Telemetry export; real-time streaming is not required for operational decisions.
open_risks: Safety Risk: If Safety Services anomaly detection latency requirement is < 100 ms and Telemetry export is the only channel, local safety logic on Vehicle is mandatory. Current design assumes this is feasible; verification required.

Integration Risk: If flow measurement requires synchronization across multiple distributed sensors (e.g., inlet and outlet flow for net throughput), Vehicle IM boundary must include aggregation logic and inter-sensor timing synchronization. Current design assumes single-point measurement; clarification needed.

State Management Risk: Evaluation period state transitions (reset, extend, terminate) during active measurement cycles could cause data loss or inconsistency if not carefully sequenced. Atomic state machine design and Tasking interface semantics must be rigorously defined.

Calibration Risk: If sensor calibration drift occurs during evaluation period, cumulative throughput accuracy degrades. Mitigation: define calibration interval, drift monitoring, and acceptance criterion tolerance to account for drift.

Telemetry Coupling Risk: If Safety Services or NGHE Solution implement real-time feedback loops based on Telemetry flow rate data, latency and jitter in export could cause control instability. Mitigation: define acceptable latency bounds and implement buffering/smoothing on consumer side.

Hardware Ownership Risk: If flow sensor hardware is procured or maintained by external party (e.g., third-party equipment provider), Vehicle IM boundary definition and interface contract must be explicit to avoid ambiguity in responsibility for calibration, replacement, and failure diagnosis.
evidence_needed: Worksite operational policy document specifying: productivity_target, evaluation period definition, acceptable measurement tolerance (±%), sensor calibration interval, and anomaly thresholds (e.g., flow rate range, rate-of-change limits).
Safety Services requirements specification: latency bounds for anomaly detection, alert priority levels, and acceptable false-positive/false-negative rates.
Sensor hardware specification and mounting diagram: confirm all transducers, signal conditioning circuits, and ADCs are Vehicle-resident or establish external interface contract.
Tasking interface specification: define evaluation period command semantics (reset, extend, terminate), calibration command format, and state transition rules.
Telemetry interface specification: define export frequency, latency bounds, data format, and priority queuing for alerts vs. telemetry data.
Numerical integration algorithm specification: document integration method (e.g., trapezoidal, Simpson's rule), error bounds, and numerical stability analysis for expected flow rate ranges.
Safety-critical anomaly detection algorithm: specify threshold values, detection latency, and local alert generation logic on Vehicle firmware.
Calibration procedure and traceability: document calibration standard, reference equipment, acceptance criteria, and state persistence mechanism.
